myths.Agent_1:
Before the world had voices, the tall mountains stood in absolute silence, heavy and brooding. Aelys, the youngest daughter of the West Wind, loved to dance among the jagged peaks. Unlike her sisters who howled and tore at the trees, Aelys gathered the sounds of the world—the rustle of leaves, the babble of brooks, the songs of early birds—and carried them in a woven pouch of starlight.

One twilight, she discovered a lonely, sleeping mountain giant whose heart was made of hollow stone. He wept in his sleep, his tears carving deep ravines, because he could not hear the beauty of the world below. Moved by his sorrow, Aelys opened her pouch and poured the gathered sounds into the giant's hollow chest. 

Awakening, the giant laughed, a booming sound that suddenly shook the earth. But the beautiful sounds were trapped within his stone ribs. To free them, Aelys struck an eternal bargain: whenever a traveler called out to the mountains, the giant would catch the sound in his rocky heart and throw it back, sharing the song of the world forever. Thus, the first Echo was born, a lasting gift of the wind to the weeping stone.

Though Aethon was consumed by the abyss, his scattered feathers remained. To this day, they twinkle in the night sky as stars, a reminder of the great bird who sacrificed his light so the world would never succumb to the dark.

Myth: When the Great Luminary Aethon was dragged into the deep abyss by the serpent Moros, the world was plunged into a heavy, unbroken silence. The sky was left with only the cold pricks of his fallen feathers—the stars—and the sorrowful pale moon. The earth below grew hollow and wept in the dark.

Zephyrus, the gentle morning wind, refused to let Aethon’s legacy fade into absolute shadow. Roaming the desolate forests, Zephyrus found small, trembling creatures of dull grey hiding in the barren trees. They had no voices and no hope.

Reaching up to the night sky, Zephyrus gathered the residual starlight—the last echoes of Aethon’s radiant warmth—and placed a single spark into the throat of each dull creature. 

Instantly, the creatures transformed. Their drab feathers burst into vibrant hues of blue, crimson, and gold. As they opened their beaks, the trapped starlight poured out not as fire, but as a brilliant, cascading melody. 

To this day, these tiny descendants of Aethon’s light gather just before the sun rises. They sing the Dawn Chorus, throwing their radiant songs into the dark sky to chase away Moros's shadows and call the warming light back to the waking world.

She reached into the night sky and gently pulled down threads of the dancing aurora—crimson, gold, and vibrant orange. With a soft, humming breath, she wove these warm celestial lights directly into the canopy of the great forests.

Instantly, the grey leaves absorbed the celestial embers and burst into radiant, fiery hues. As the cold winds blew, the illuminated leaves detached, drifting down like falling sparks to blanket the freezing roots in a protective layer of warmth and color.

To this day, before the deep frost of winter takes hold, the trees remember Aelys’s gift. They shed their green to display the captive fires of the sky, creating the season of Autumn as a fleeting echo of the brilliant northern lights.
